text: Welcome to another video. Today we're going to be reviewing a technique that's become more commonly utilized by hernia surgeons, primary abandonment of the sac for inguinal scrotal hernias. This is a four-year-old man who presented with a symptomatic inguinal scrotal hernia, no prior surgical history. and a hernia that was easily reducible in clinic. Given the circumstances, I didn't think further preoperative imaging was indicated.

We plan to move forward with a robotic inguinal hernia repair with primary abandonment of the sac, a proactive approach to minimize hematomas and trauma to the cord elements. So what is primary abandonment of the sac? It's when you incise the peritoneum around the deep ring. and address that fenestration at the completion of the case during peritoneal flap closure. I think it minimizes bleeding and tissue trauma related to sac dissection around the cord elements, can reduce operating room time variability.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum circumferentially around the deep ring, creating a large opening and leaving the distal sac behind as part of the primary abandonment technique.

The surgeon initially uses electrocautery with high thermal settings, causing excessive thermal spread, and then transitions to short dissection to prevent injury to nearby vasculature and nerves. The abandonment of the sac involves a partial incision, avoiding a complete circular cut due to difficulty visualizing the medial aspect, which will be addressed during the peritoneal flap creation.

The surgeon continues the dissection in the parietal compartment down to the pubis, followed by lateral dissection in the visceral compartment. The previously created fenestration is encountered, and the surgeon begins finalizing the circumferential incision around the neck of the deep ring.

The surgeon demonstrates the parietalization or lateralization of the vessels, ensuring that only the peritoneum is included in the dissection. The surgical field shows minor micro bleeding episodes during the procedure.

The surgeon finalizes the circumferential incision of the sac, completely dissociating the peritoneum from the distal sac. The procedure transitions to a standard inguinal hernia repair, focusing on parietalization of the cord elements, including the vas. An adequate pocket is created for an extra-large heavyweight mesh, specifically a 3D Max heavyweight mesh, to maximize the tissue-mesh interface.

The surgeon performs dissection in the medial area to achieve a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, further parietalizing the vas. Prominent vessels are visible around the obturator region. The lateral dissection is finalized to accommodate the placement of an extra-large mesh.

The surgical field shows minor oozing as the surgeon prepares to close the peritoneal flap. An extra large mesh is positioned within the space created, fitting well. The surgeon plans to close the peritoneal incision and address a fenestration near the hernia neck.

The surgeon observes the hernia neck with no tension on the flap closure, performed under 15 mmHg pressure. The mesh is seen lying flat against the abdominal wall, providing adequate coverage at the lateral corner. The surgical field shows a smooth and satisfying outcome with no visible complications.
